flashcards.front: What is the primary purpose of control variables in experimental design?
flashcards.back: Control variables maintain constant conditions to isolate the effect of independent variables on dependent variables.
Randomization randomly assigns subjects to groups to minimize selection bias and distribute confounding variables equally.

flashcards.front: What distinguishes factorial designs from simple experiments?
flashcards.back: Factorial designs simultaneously investigate multiple independent variables and their interactions.

flashcards.front: What does a null hypothesis state?
flashcards.back: A null hypothesis claims no significant relationship exists between studied variables.

flashcards.front: Why is blinding used in experiments?
flashcards.back: Blinding prevents participants/researchers from influencing results through conscious/unconscious bias.

flashcards.front: What does ANOVA test in experimental analysis?
flashcards.back: ANOVA (Analysis of Variance) tests whether means of multiple groups differ statistically.

flashcards.front: What is blocking in experimental design?
flashcards.back: Blocking groups similar experimental units to reduce variability within groups.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of pilot studies?
flashcards.back: Pilot studies test experimental procedures and identify flaws before full-scale implementation.

flashcards.front: Differentiate Type I vs Type II errors.
flashcards.back: Type I: False positive (reject true null). Type II: False negative (fail to reject false null).
Covariates are variables measured but not controlled, used to adjust data analysis.
Quasi-experiments lack random assignment but study real-world interventions.
Internal validity measures how well an experiment establishes causal relationships.
Operationalization defines abstract concepts into measurable variables.
Interaction effects occur when the effect of one variable depends on another variable.
